title: Invitation for the first lecture in the series on "Being & Thought in/of (Inner) Asia: Understanding Epochs of Asian Lives in Pre-Common Era"
author: oxfordpublicphilosophy
raw_article:
Dear opp friends

We invite you to join us as Dr Liu Xinru speaks on “Languages and Art, How the Kushans Communicated in an Urban Setting” and discusses with Dr Deven Patel as an opening lecture in the series Understanding Epochs of Asian Lives: Being and Thought in Pre-Common Era Inner Asia.

Date and Time: Feb 16, 2025 - 7 PM IST/8.30 EST/GMT-5

Registration: https://forms.gle/uDPrQ9ebmXBT4e7n9

Speaker: Xinru Liu is Professor Emeritus of History at the College of New Jersey. Her previous books include The Silk Road in World History and Ancient India and Ancient China: Trade and Religious Exchanges, A.D. 1–600. Her most recent work is "Dionysus and drama in the Buddhist art of Gandhara," written jointly with Pia Brancaccio and published in the Journal of Global History.

Discussant: Deven M. Patel is an associate professor of Sanskrit language and the literatures of South Asia at the University of Pennsylvania. His published works include Text to Tradition: The Naiṣadhīyacarita and Literary Community in South Asia, 2014, and Kavirājamārgam: The Way of the King of Poets (co-authored with R.V.S. Sundaram), 2017.

Topic: “Languages and Art, How the Kushans Communicated in an Urban Setting” in the philosophy lecture series Being and Thought in Inner Asia: Understanding Epochs of Asian Lives in Pre-Common Era. (More about the topic can be learnt here).

About the series: “Understanding The Epochs of Asian Lives,” is a broad and multi-series philosophical project by a team from Let’s Talk Tattva (LTT) and Oxford Public Philosophy Journal (OPP). This project aims to explore a philosophical understanding of what constitutes the dense mass of “Being” and “Thought” (or literature and philosophy, when seen in their beyond-academia inclusive sense) of Asian lives when studied on interrelated historical and geographical axes. It strives to learn and acquaint how the five major geographical constituents of Asia (Central/Inner Asia, Middle East-North Africa (MENA), South Asia, East Asia, and South-East Asia) developed their philosophical understandings and how it led to what ensued after each epoch in their lives.

Thanks and hexes,

LTT & OPP

title: opp is urgently fundraising!
author: oxfordpublicphilosophy
raw_article:
hello opp friends,

we hope you are well and having a good start to this new earthly revolution. may it be filled with philosophical joys and mischief!

oxford public philosophy is urgently fundraising to cover its annual website costs of £204 (plus applicable taxes)!

as you know, opp is an online platform whose goal is to question what philosophy is and how we are doing it, both in form and content. we welcome work that investigates received assumptions about the nature and aims of philosophical inquiry, including contributions that question structural norms of currently dominant research programmes. our website features four journal issues (or “turns”), an open forum, and an online classroom.

we are a student-led, non-profit organization. as such, we rely on small grants and donations to continue fulfilling our mission. if you like our work, please don’t hesitate to make a donation!

the easiest way to support opp is to become a paid substack subscriber. subscription options include monthly subscriptions of £5 per month, annual subscriptions of £30 per year, and founding member subscriptions of over £30 per year.

Subscribe now

other payment options include PayPal and bank transfers. you will find below our banking details.

if you have any questions, you can email us at oxfordpublicphilosophy@gmail.com.

we wholeheartedly thank you for your support!

with grateful hexes,
the opp team

title: opp is recruiting!
author: oxfordpublicphilosophy
raw_article:
hello friends,

you want to join the opp team or know someone who would be interested in getting involved with us? opp is recruiting for its next turns! all and any welcome to apply! applicants do not need to be oxford students.

the following positions are currently open for applications:

  • two co-organisers: co-organisers guide the direction of opp and assist other team members in their tasks. they have the opportunity to organise the team, liaise with and create an expansive and inclusive community around the globe, and develop our educational projects in line with our mission to uplift voices to transform philosophy and our world.

  • a journal coordinator: the journal coordinator guides the direction of the journal by creating a timeline for the journal team, making sure all roles are fulfilled as needed, ensuring section leads carry out responsibilities throughout the year, and making sure all journal contents materials are prepared on time and in requested format for the creative team to compile. the journal coordinator also takes a leading role in organising the launch event and creating development goals with the co-organisers, section leads, and treasurer.

  • prose editors: prose editors edit the prose submissions. they work under the guidance of the prose lead and attend the editorial meetings organized by the prose lead. They send the edited pieces on time and follow the timeline suggested by the prose lead and journal coordinator.

  • poetry editors: poetry editors edit the poetry submissions. they work under the guidance of the poetry lead and attend the editorial meetings organized by the poetry lead. They send the edited pieces on time and follow the timeline suggested by the poetry lead and journal coordinator.

  • a communications officer: the communications officer promotes opp’s projects and events by writing a monthly newsletter, sending out emails to academic institutions, and advertising opp’s activities on social media (facebook, instagram, twitter, and linkedin). the communications officer works closely with the co-organisers, the journal coordinator, the blog team members, and the events team members to ensure our promotion needs are met.

  • open forum team members: the open forum team members are in charge of reviewing submissions for our blog and review forum, liaising with authors, and writing a call for submissions if needed. they also work with the co-organisers and communications officer on promoting the open forum.

for more information about any role and to apply, email oxfordpublicphilosophy@gmail.com with your CV and a statement of interest anytime.

hexes,
the opp team

title: turn 5 call for submissions - a reminder!
author: oxfordpublicphilosophy
raw_article:
hello friends,

with our fifth earthly revolution around the sun, opp invites you to submit your philosophical compositions iteration for ‘turn 5’ of our annual philosophy journal. we welcome prosaic, poetic, artistic, a meeting thereof, or other-formly pieces focusing on any one or combination of our turn 5 themes:

  1. philosophies of the Indigenous peoples of Oceania

  2. cosmological/gonical and conceptual schemes

  3. artificial intelligence

with these themes we invite you to intrepidly, carefully explore our interactions within or between amixt any of our themes. with these themes we invite you to inspect redraw move about play with:

logics

language

symbols

lines

time and space

surroundings

embodiment

consciousness

and their limitations.

we invite you to transgress and question, to analyse, to uphold, to overturn definitions or bolster the unexpected, to spin out poetic possibilities of hermeneutic care, to link our themes of 4 with 5, to mind colonial occupation, reclamation, rematriation, stewardship, reverence.

we invite contributing participants to submit poetry, argumentative or creative prose, artwork or anything in between to explore one or many of these sub-themes in dialogical acts to expand philosophy’s field of concern, in form and in content, as we encounter it from our various positions in the world.

we invite submissions in three categories: art, poetry, and prose, though contributors should feel free to work over and around these boundaries. multimedia submissions are welcome.

please click here for specific guidelines and submission portals for each category.

deadline for submissions is 15 SEPTEMBER 2024.

any questions should be sent to oxfordpublicphilosophy@gmail.com.

keep in touch

opp is always looking for collaborators, contributors, and team members!

you can write something for the opp blog, including reviews (of books, shows, &c), retorts, and anything else that critically reflects on what philosophy is and how we're doing it.

if you like what we do, support us on paypal here.

follow us on social media for more regular updates & interactions.

hexes,
the opp team

title: a conversation with Joy James!
subtitle: inviting you to speak with the philosopher Joy James this thursday ❤️
author: oxfordpublicphilosophy
raw_article:
hey lovely people xx

we are so excited to offer you the chance to join us this thursday 5-7pm in conversation with the brilliant and inspiring Joy James!

Joy is a political philosopher, well known for her incredible theories and written works on feminism, critical race theory, political prisoners, democracy and social justice.

read more about Joy here!

her writing is remarkable, speaking candidly about the reality of our lives and the social world we have constructed, in all of its beauty and terror. In care, she reminds us how to learn, how to recreate and where the good life truly lies.

check out her many books!

with recent difficulties getting to Oxford - this event is now being hosted online, which is wonderful on account that we can open up this free event to absolutely everyone!

please feel free to share this link with all and anyone - we would love to show Joy all our support, and share her message with the world.

hope to see you then xx

love,

opp

a chance to meet and speak with Joy James!

an event in Oxford, with the American political philosopher, academic, and author.

her areas are, though not limited to… 

  • Political theory

  • Human rights

  • Antiblackness and genocide

  • Feminisms

  • Abolitionisms

what? a conversation/book talk, with Q/a

when: 245th April

where: tbd! 

Joy James is Ebenezer Fitch Professor of Humanities at Williams College. 

Her most recent book is In Pursuit of Revolutionary Love: Precarity, Power, Communities. 

She is editor of The Angela Y. Davis Reader, Imprisoned Intellectuals, The New Abolitionists, and Warfare in the American Homeland. She is also author of Resisting State Violence, Transcending the Talented Tenth, Seeking the Beloved Community, New Bones Abolition: Captive Maternal Agency and the Afterlife of Erica Garner, and the forthcoming Contextualizing Angela Davis.

James has published numerous articles on: political theory, police, prison and slavery abolition; radicalizing feminisms; diasporic anti-black racism; and US politics; and writes on the Captive Maternal through the lens of “The Womb of Western Theory.”

an invitation to Joy James’ upcoming virtual event!

[on behalf of your being human, the following invitation has been extended to you]

Dear Human:

We are excited to extend to you a cordial invitation to register for our upcoming virtual conference: “Resolving the Race/ism Dilemma.” Join us for this interactive virtual event on Friday, April 5th (2024) from 7-8:30 PM EST and Saturday, April 6th (2024) from 9 AM-3 PM EST. This event promises to be a significant milestone in our shared journey toward understanding and dismantling the belief in “race” and the reality of racism.

In a world still wrestling with the enduring impacts of race/ism, this conference serves as a crucial platform for unraveling the complex layers that constitute “race” and racism. It is an exceptional opportunity to engage with leading voices advocating for a critical examination of race/ism through the transformative lenses of racelessness, antiracialization, and deracialization.

The “Resolving the Race/ism Dilemma” conference will feature in-depth discussions challenging the traditional notions of “race” as an immutable aspect of human identity. We will explore how social constructions of racism (i.e., race/ism) and historical contingencies have shaped our perceptions of so-called racial differences, fueling and upholding inequalities. Our collective exploration will not only question how so-called racial identities are imposed but also celebrate the fluidity and richness of cultural, ethnic, individual, and collective diversity beyond so-called racial categorizations.

By fostering transformative dialogues, we aim to envision and contribute to the creation of a society that ends race/ism, celebrating the shared humanity of all its members. This event is not merely a conference; it is a commitment to a more inclusive, just, and understanding world.

We believe your participation is essential in this vital conversation. Your commitment to exploring the complexities of “race,” racism, and the potential for “racial” eliminativism is commendable and crucial to our collective progress.

For more details and to register, please visit: https://www.eventcreate.com/e/resolving-the-raceism-dilemma.

We look forward to your participation in this transformative event.

the lovely Sheena Mason, author of the Theory of Racelessness: A Case for Antirace(ism)!

✵ ✵ ✵

that’s it!

and we thank you for your engagement with public philosophy ❤️

the opp team

an invitation to Joy James’ upcoming virtual event!
[on behalf of your being human, the following invitation has been extended to you]
Dear Human:

We are excited to extend to you a cordial invitation to register for our upcoming virtual conference: “Resolving the Race/ism Dilemma.” Join us for this interactive virtual event on Friday, April 5th(2024) from 7-8:30 PM EST and Saturday, April 6th(2024) from 9 AM-3 PM EST. This event promises to be a significant milestone in our shared journey toward understanding and dismantling the belief in “race” and the reality of racism.

In a world still wrestling with the enduring impacts of race/ism, this conference serves as a crucial platform for unraveling the complex layers that constitute “race” and racism. It is an exceptional opportunity to engage with leading voices advocating for a critical examination of race/ism through the transformative lenses of racelessness, antiracialization, and deracialization.

The “Resolving the Race/ism Dilemma” conference will feature in-depth discussions challenging the traditional notions of “race” as an immutable aspect of human identity. We will explore how social constructions of racism (i.e., race/ism) and historical contingencies have shaped our perceptions of so-called racial differences, fueling and upholding inequalities. Our collective exploration will not only question how so-called racial identities are imposed but also celebrate the fluidity and richness of cultural, ethnic, individual, and collective diversity beyond so-called racial categorizations.

By fostering transformative dialogues, we aim to envision and contribute to the creation of a society that ends race/ism, celebrating the shared humanity of all its members. This event is not merely a conference; it is a commitment to a more inclusive, just, and understanding world.

We believe your participation is essential in this vital conversation. Your commitment to exploring the complexities of “race,” racism, and the potential for “racial” eliminativism is commendable and crucial to our collective progress.
For more details and to register, please visit:https://www.eventcreate.com/e/resolving-the-raceism-dilemma.
We look forward to your participation in this transformative event.
the lovely Sheena Mason, author of the Theory of Racelessness: A Case for Antirace(ism)!
that’s it!
and we thank you for your  engagement with public philosophy ❤️

title: Free event: Power, politics and Indigenous ecological knowledge of South American featherworks
subtitle: extending an invitation to tomorrow's event!
author: oxfordpublicphilosophy
raw_article:
hello friends!

opp invites you to join us and Join Rosa Dyer (AHRC Collaborative Doctoral Partnership researcher) for a tour at the Pitt Rivers Museum tomorrow! where we will be exploring what feathered objects can teach us about the dynamic relationships held between people, birds and environments. We will explore examples in the current Pitt Rivers displays to think about how feathered artefacts, alongside face paint and music, are being used as tools for Indigenous empowerment in fights against biodiversity loss, threats to Indigenous cultural heritage and the defence of territorial rights. 

Date and time: Wednesday February, 28th 2pm

Location: Pitt Rivers Museum (base of the stairs at the entrance)

you can email emily.zhai@stx.ox.ac.uk if you cannot find the event!

Bio: Rosa is an AHRC Collaborative Doctoral Project student at Birkbeck College, University of London and the Pitt Rivers Museum, University of Oxford. She is a member of the Ethno-Ornithology World Atlas Lab at Oxford. Her research is based in the field of ethnobiology, and works collaboratively with Indigenous ecologists, activists and artists to explore the South American featherwork collections held at the Pitt Rivers Museum.

with love,

we hope to see you there!

opp

title: opp is launching its turn 4!
author: oxfordpublicphilosophy
raw_article:
opp (oxfordpublicphilosophy.com) invites you to the launch event for its fourth annual journal, or TURN 4, dedicated to:

  • the Indigenous philosophies of northern America

  • environment

  • sentience

******************************************************************

join us OVER ZOOM

February 29th
7:00 pm – 8:30 pm UK time

https://us06web.zoom.us/j/81562319401?pwd=lqedIQDjlceWbD4NPqEFhJ2BY85sMa.1 ID: 815 6231 9401

Secret code: 318057

for an evening of art, poetry, and philosophical discussion
with mingling, with reasoning, with beings questioning the way we do philosophy both in form and content…
to transgress, question, analyze, uphold, or overturn received assumptions about the nature and aims of philosophical inquiry.

all welcome.

SPEAKERS:

Perry Ground

John Shoptaw

Briana Muñoz

Oscar Horta

******************************************************************

SPEAKERS BIOS

Perry Ground is a Turtle Clan member of the Onondaga Nation and has been telling stories for more than 25 years as a way of educating people about the culture, beliefs and history of the Haudenosaunee (sometimes known as Iroquois) Confederacy. Perry shares his stories in a very energetic, fun, and engaging style and makes the audience part of the story experience. Also an accomplished Educator, Perry has worked with students from Pre-K through college. The classroom programs that Perry offers are taught in the same engaging style and enhance each student’s study of Native Peoples. Perry is available to visit schools, museums, libraries, festivals, and more to share stories and programs that will educate and entertain audiences of all ages.

John Shoptaw is a poet, essayist, teacher, and environmentalist.  He teaches poetry and environmental poetry & poetics at UC Berkeley, where he is a member of the Environmental Arts & Humanities Initiative.  Shoptaw’s first poetry collection, Times Beach (Notre Dame Press, 2015), won the Notre Dame Review Book Prize and subsequently also the 2016 Northern California Book Award in Poetry. Shoptaw focuses on contemporary experience: on what it means to live and write among other creatures in a world deranged by human-caused climate change.  These questions are also at the center of his essays “Why Ecopoetry?” (published in 2016 at Poetry Magazine, where a number of his poems, including “Near-Earth Object,” have also appeared) and “The Poetry of Our Climate” (forthcoming at American Poetry Review).  

Briana Muñoz is a poet from Southern California. She is the author of two books of poetry including Loose Lips (Prickly Pear Publishing) and Everything is Returned to the Soil (FlowerSong Press). Her work has been published in the anthology How to Reimagine America, Cultural Daily, the Beat Not Beat Anthology, the Oakland Arts Review, Dryland Literary Journal, the Angel City Review, the Somos Xicanas anthology, and several other publications. Briana is the founder of Poetry as Harm Reduction and currently serving as the board of directors secretary for the Los Angeles Poet Society. She has performed poetry in places like the International Poetry Festival of Havana, Cuba, the 2015 Festival de Poesia in Tijuana, Baja California, MX, as well as at the XXI Congreso Internacional de Literatura y Estudios Hispanicos in Quito, Ecuador.

Oscar Horta is a philosopher at the University of Santiago de Compostela, where he leads the Wild Animal Ethics project. He's also the co-founder of the charity Animal Ethics and the author of Making a Stand for Animals (Routledge, 2022).

******************************************************************

THE TURN 4 JOURNAL TEAM

journal coordination: Virginie Simoneau-Gilbert

prose co-leads: Beatrice Marchegiani and Virginie Simoneau-Gilbert

poetry lead: Bri Jurries

journal design and graphics: Ellen Potter

hexes,

the opp team

title: new book reviews, a featured philosopher & upcoming journal launch!
subtitle: your slice of student-run public philosophy this week!
author: oxfordpublicphilosophy
raw_article:
hello wonderful people x

a very happy new year, and amongst the chaos all around us, we wish you toasty winter days and clear starry nights.

✵ ✵ ✵

what this newsletter covers:

✩ a new featured philosopher: Lacey J Davidson

✩ a new book review: The Bright Side of Dark Moods

✩ an upcoming journal launch (stay tuned!)

✩ join us: it’s that time for recruiting again!

✵ ✵ ✵

a new featured philosopher

as part of our online classroom, opp wants to continue its endeavours highlighting philosophers across time and space who deserve more of our attention! amongst our other libraries (including for example historical women political thinkers and African(a) philosophies), our featured philosopher page shares with you some brilliant minds of our modern age, such as;

Lacey J Davidson

who offers a crucial examination of one fundamental aspect in the ongoing battle against marginalisation and oppression: epistemic responsibility.

"Are we responsible for our knowledge and how we act on that knowledge? Can we be responsible for what we don't know? Are we responsible when we act on biases of which we are unaware?" (p. 174).

read it here, by our brilliant writer and team member, Silvia Paciaroni

✵ ✵ ✵

a new book review! definitely worth reading.

thank you to Ayushi Jolly, a PhD Scholar in Social Psychology at Jawaharlal Nehru University, India, who brings beautiful insights and discussion to Mariana Alessandri’s Night Vision: Seeing Ourselves Through Dark Moods.

shining light on the “rabbit hole of the billion-dollar industry of Self-help and Pop Psychology,” “the blind promotion of positivity,” the “gap between psychiatric and societal narratives of mental illnesses” - and so much more.

what is toxic positivity? how is it bred, and how does it harm us?

read her brilliant piece here

✵ ✵ ✵

an upcoming journal launch…

get excited! turn 4 is on its way!

in case you missed it, this turn’s themes are:

the environment, sentience, and the philosophies of indigenous peoples of North America.

with almost 30 pieces of beautiful poetry, prose, and artwork (including fantastic Haudenosaunee storytelling) - we are beyond excited to publish this online journal!

we expect it to be launched Feb 20th and stay tuned! our launch event this year will be online so everyone can join! come listen to some of our contributors speak about their work and give talks on these three topics and their intersections; from academics to artists, poets to philosophers.

✵ ✵ ✵

recruiting, recruiting, recruiting

once again, it is that time for recruitment to open! we are a student-run free educational platform and space for all. as students come and go, and our amazing team members move on to bigger and better things, we welcome new faces!

we look forward to hearing your ideas and growing with you. we offer you a space to bring your creativity and love for philosophy, in all forms and content!

currently a team of 15 students, mostly based in Oxford but all over the globe, from India to America! we are undergraduates, masters and PhD students, and diverse in our range of studies (spoiler alert! not all of us study philosophy)

are we looking for you?

✩ would you like to review our art submissions and liaise with incredible artists?

apply to be our art lead!!!

✩ would you like to review our poetry submissions and practice your hand at editing?

apply to be one of our poetry editors!

✩ would you like to learn how to monetise content, from youtube videos to book commissions?

apply to be our treasurer!

✩ would you like to organise and host events, from philosophy socials to poetry nights, to book talks and panel discussions? and, the opportunity to liaise with incredible academics, artists and authors across the globe?

apply to be our co-events lead, with the incredible Emily Zhai!

else, we are always open to hearing and adding more roles. if you have something special to bring to public philosophy, pitch it and don’t be afraid to reach out!

then email us at oxfordpublicphilosophy@gmail.com!

✵ ✵ ✵

we wish you all the best.

and we warmly thank you for your interest in public philosophy.

love, the opp team.

Share oxford public philosophy newsletter

as a free, student-run platform, we welcome any and all charitable gifts. we are currently raising money to invite an incredible native storyteller to speak at our launch, as well as saving up money to fund our website subscription and hopefully more in-person events.

Give a gift subscription

raw_article:
hello lovely people x

as the oxford term comes to an end and the cold begins to bite, opp wishes you a warm winter and a wonderful holiday, filled with lovely people and self-care.

looking forward, we are excited for the year ahead! this break, opp will continue to collate and put together a new page on our website, offering resources on the humanitarian crisis in Gaza as discussed in our last newsletter (if you missed it and are interested, here is the link, which includes a dozen or so resources so far). we will also be finalising and wrapping up our term 4 journal, which we are beyond excited about! which will be on the themes of the philosophies of indigenous peoples of north america, environment and sentience. stay tuned!

*ੈ✩‧₊˚༺ ☆ ༻*ੈ✩‧₊˚

newsletter summary:

☆ opp: who are we & what do we do?

☆ winter fundraising call: keep our free public journal alive!

☆ recruitment call: join the team opp team!

*ੈ✩‧₊˚༺ ☆ ༻*ੈ✩‧₊˚

oxford public philosophy: who are we?

what do we do?

opp is a space for critically questioning what philosophy is & how we are doing it, in form and content.

ie. opp offers a blog and a review forum - places to critically question and discuss philosophy from a place outside institutional boundaries - open to submission from the public!

☆ check out our submission guidelines if you’d like to engage with public philosophy on opp!

opp welcomes work that investigates received assumptions about the nature and aims of philosophical inquiry, including contributions that question structural norms of currently dominant research programmes.

ie. opp offers a free public library of resources compiled by academics in universities across the world, on diverse topics beyond the narrow limits of an oxford philosophy degree!

☆ check out our resource library here (on featured philosophers, historical women political thinkers, African (a) philosophies, south asian philosophies, philosophers racialised as black, and insurrectionist ethics) our newest piece is on a featured philosopher lacey davidson!

opp will contribute to rigorous discussion of these matters in part through openness to different genres, styles and themes.

ie. opp seeks to open public philosophy up to a world outside norms, by engaging all forms of expression - we discuss philosophy through writing, through poetry, art, music, in podcasts, youtube videos, and so on!

the aim is to make room for the possibility of strengthening, broadening or contesting our interpretative frameworks and field of consideration.

*ੈ✩‧₊˚༺ ☆ ༻*ੈ✩‧₊˚

winter fundraising call

please help us keep our site alive! any amount helps us reach our goal, and allows us to continue providing free resources and a public platform for unique philosophical engagement.

opp asks for your support today to help keep our website alive, as the central platform for all our free content, including the upcoming journal. to ensure accessibility, opp does not charge for admission to events or any online contents and is not directly affiliated with any institutions in order to maintain our programme and team-care integrity. this does mean we need to rely on grants and individual donations to support all the volunteer work we do in addition to our student and job work. please help us to meet our 250 GBP goal to keep our website going!

as students come and go, opp has several roles available and will be searching for some wonderful people to join our team this coming year!

the opp team

oxford public philosophy conjures its various modes of public critical examination as a team – in this case, striving for a non-hierarchical organising collective. philosophising with care includes organising structures with care and cooperation.

we are currently looking for:

☆ new co-organisers

☆ a funding team (especially a treasurer)

☆ a journal coordinator

☆ a prose editor

☆ a poetry editor

☆ artists

if you are interested in joining us, please email your cv to us and a statement of interest (which role(s) you might be keen on) to oxfordpublicphilosophy@gmail.com.

thank you!

opp wishes you a wonderful break and thanks you for your engagement with public philosophy, pushing the boundaries of how we do philosophy one mind at a time.

love, the opp team

title: resources on the humanitarian crisis in Gaza ✩
subtitle: opp stands in solidarity for a cease fire now.
author: oxfordpublicphilosophy
raw_article:
dear friends of opp around the world,

at the march demanding an end to the atrocities being committed in Gaza on saturday 18th november 2023, a sign read, “a textbook case of genocide.”

in my first year as an undergraduate student in the usa, i took a course on the anthropology of violence with a focus on genocide. the crisis in Gaza is literally a textbook case of genocide, and accusations of war crimes and the breaking of international conventions made to prevent genocide are not the sole sanctions for calling for a cease fire. we do not need these definitions to tell us that the continued crisis in Gaza is horrifying and the killing of children, the injured, and ill, of civilians, is almost beyond belief.

the opp team supports a cease fire now. we encourage you to speak out and engage with the complex histories involved. we are providing resources from our new circle of advisors and beyond in the coming weeks and permanently soon in our online library.

if you would like to share information with us or to write or create a philosophy piece, please email us for consideration. pieces will be referred to trusted experts for review.

calling for a cease fire now.

in solidarity with all those struggling with the events in Gaza,

the opp coven

✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩

RESOURCES:

opp reached out to our new circle of advisors - a group of philosophers, academics, thinkers, and scholar-activists we work with from all over the world - to share nuanced and educational resources on the ongoing humanitarian crisis in Gaza.

we recognise that it is difficult to find critical histories. we understand it is hard to trust in a fragmented world. we ask you to engage with these resources we share with your own critical eye. furthermore, we encourage you to reflect quietly and discuss communally if you are not already. to challenge your own preconceptions. we ask you to work towards a beloved community of integrity, to recognise entanglement, and to cultivate deep compassion. we ask you to look to all traditions of the world and peoples, especially those less heard and to find those silenced, for wisdom not just solace. let this email be a start or a continuation. more to come on our website, social media, and YouTube page soon. thank you.

although we had envisioned sharing our circle of advisors under different circumstances, we instead today thank them for sharing resources with us in an act of public philosophy aspiring to safer, much much more liveable, and cherished transformed world. thus we thank our advisors, in solidarity: Alice Crary, Alex Watson, Anthony Morgan, Anthony Neal, Carol J. Adams, Greg Moses, Henry Giroux, Jane Anna Gordon, Jessica Frazier, Jonardon Ganeri, Joy James, Lee A. McBride III, Nayef Al-Rodhan, Paul Lodge, and Shay Welch

✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩

An Open Letter from Oxford Academics

an open letter on the humanitarian crisis in Gaza, written and signed by a group of academics who are scholars of political science, political philosophy, ethics, history, geography, law and the Middle East - including Alice Crary, a member of opp’s circle of advisors.

“We implore you, as academics who spend our lives thinking about events such as these, to see what, in the fullness of history, will be obvious to all: that Israel is today engaged in a morally disastrous exercise, and that those nations who give Israel cover to do so have innocent Palestinian blood on their hands. In the name of human dignity and moral decency, you must call for an immediate ceasefire in Gaza.”

read the full letter here

body.content: dear friends ofopparound the world,
at the march demanding an end to the atrocities being committed in Gaza on saturday 18th november 2023, a sign read, “a textbook case of genocide.”

in my first year as an undergraduate student in the usa, i took a course on the anthropology of violence with a focus on genocide. the crisis in Gaza isliterallya textbook case of genocide, and accusations of war crimes and the breaking of international conventions made to prevent genocide are not the sole sanctions for calling for a cease fire. we do not need these definitions to tell us that the continued crisis in Gaza is horrifying and the killing of children, the injured, and ill, of civilians, is almost beyond belief.

theoppteam supports a cease fire now. we encourage you to speak out and engage with the complex histories involved. we are providing resources from our new circle of advisors and beyond in the coming weeks and permanently soon in our online library.
if you would like to share information with us or to write or create a philosophy piece, please email us for consideration. pieces will be referred to trusted experts for review.
calling for a cease fire now.
in solidarity with all those struggling with the events in Gaza,
theoppcoven

hello lovely people!

we hope you’ve had a wonderful week;

here are some upcoming philosophy-related events that you might be interested in!

Summary:

✩ lecture on the 17th nov. on epistemology, through a Buddhist (Nyaya) lens.

✩ applications open! Caribbean Philosophical Association Summer School

✩ We remember the attempts to be free: writing by James Jones

✩ Oxford’s open letter to Gaza: read

✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩

Connecting the Epistemology of Nyaya to 20th Century Analytic Epistemology

Week 6, Friday 17th November, 2.00-3.00
OCHS Library / Zoom
Prof. Anand Jayprakash Vaidya

This lecture will present how and why it is important to study cross-cultural epistemology. I will focus on Nyaya and Analytic epistemology, especially Gangesha, the Oxford Realists, such as H.A. Pritchard and T. Williamson, the Los Angeles Externalist, T. Burge, and the Pittsburgh Disjunctvist, J. McDowell. I will discuss three topics. First, the analysis of knowledge and justification. Second, the philosophy of perception. Third, the relevance of the theory of certification in Nyaya as an intervention into Analytic epistemology. Along the way I will suggest some revisions to Gangesha so as to update how his theory can engage contemporary epistemology.

Prof. Anand Jayprakash Vaidya is Professor in the Department of Philosophy, San Jose State University. Among his research interests is the epistemology of modality or how we come to know what is possible and necessary for the variety of kinds of particulars that there are. He defends an epistemological approach to show how we can know what is metaphysically possible and necessary.

✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩ ✩

CPA Summer School

Caribbean Philosophical Association Summer School

The CPA summer school is a generous and intellectually rigorous environment that understands Caribbean philosophy as a transdisciplinary form of interrogation informed by scholarly knowledges as well as by practices and artistic expressions that elucidate fundamental questions that emerge in contexts of discovery, conquest, racial, gender, and sexual domination, genocide, dependency, and exploitation as well as freedom, emancipation, and decolonization.

When? The 2023 CPA Summer School will be held from June 25th -July 2nd.

Instructors include: Hortense Spillers, Paget Henry, Nkiru Nzegwu, Oscar Guardiola Rivera, Azad Ashim Sharma, Michael Monahan, Jacqueline Martinez, Lewis R. Gordon, Katerina Gonzalez Seligmann, and Jane Anna Gordon.

Deadline to Apply: February 15th, 2024 by 11p.m. EST

read more on their website (click here!)

All the best!

title: upcoming event ✩ intersecting poetry & philosophy
author: oxfordpublicphilosophy
raw_article:
hello dear opp friends,

join us in person for our panel entitled ‘intersecting poetry and philosophy’ which will be held on November 9th, from 5 to 7 pm, in the Mary Hyde Eccles Room, Pembroke College. invited speakers who work across both genres or integrate cross-disciplinary elements in their work will discuss the resonances of poetic language and philosophy, including subjects such as religious and spiritual philosophical verse traditions, the intersection of literature and philosophy, philosophical poetry, the nature of poetic language, and the links between poetry, philosophy, and other arts.

this event is co-hosted with the Oxford University Poetry Society.

it is part of opp's fall 2023 event series based on the themes of its fourth journal:
1. philosophies of indigenous peoples of northern America
2. environment
3. sentience

poet Arch Hades, lecturer David Barnes, and philosopher Paul Lodge will discuss aspects of their own research, writing, and creative endeavours engaging in discussion to cultivate an interdisciplinary approach to the subject. the panel will be followed by a musical performance by Paul Lodge and an audience Q&A.

drinks and nibbles will be served starting from 6:20 pm.

we look forward to seeing you there!

hexes,

the opp team

*****

presentation and performance titles
‘Exploring poetry as a vehicle for philosophy’ by Arch Hades
‘Hearing the Insect City’ by David Barnes
‘Margaret Cavendish’s Poems and Fancies in Song’ by Paul Lodge

speaker bios
Arch Hades is an artist and bestselling poet from London, who became the highest paid living poet in the world in 2021. She is the author of five volumes of poetry – ‘High Tide’ (2018), ‘Fool’s Gold’ (2020), ‘Paper Romance’ (2021), ‘Arcadia’ (2022), ‘21C Human’ (2023) – known best for Romantic lyricism and for exploring poetry-of-philosophy in a traditional, rhyming style.
In 2021, ‘Arcadia’ was illustrated and sold as a work of digital fine art at Christie’s New York, where it broke records and became the most expensive poem ever sold at $525,000. In May 2022 the ‘Arcadia’ digital film had its museum premiere at the Palazzo Strozzi in Florence as part of the‘Let’s Get Digital’ exhibition.
Arch Hades often writes commentary for national newspapers on cultural discourse, performs poetry readings, and gives talks on philosophy – in 2021 speaking on 21C existentialism at Oxford University; in 2022 exploring the relationship between art and philosophy on the cultural panel at UCL’s annual conference, and discussing the themes of ‘Arcadia’ at the Cambridge Union.

David Barnes is a Departmental Lecturer in modern English Literature at Lady Margaret Hall. He specialises in Victorian and early twentieth-century writing, with major interests in the city, environmental history, and transatlantic cultural exchange. His first book, The Venice Myth: Culture, Literature, Politics 1800 to the Present (2014) explored the ways in which the built environment of Venice was reimagined by different political ideologies in the later nineteenth and twentieth centuries. His next book, White Modernities (in development) thinks about the impact of American imperialism on transatlantic modernism, and its consequences for fluid notions of race, culture and nationalism. His other major project, Urban Animals, views the emergence of modern European and North American cities through the prism of animal studies, looking at the way domestic and wild animals, pets, ‘pests’ and zoo animals shaped the ways in which urban environments were conceived in the late-Victorian and early twentieth-century periods. This was the subject of a BBC Radio 3 documentary, ‘Escape of the Zebra from the Zoo’ (2019), which he wrote and presented. He has also written for The Times, New European, Guardian, Times Literary Supplement and Literary Hub.

Paul Lodge (Professor of philosophy and Fellow of Mansfield College) sings from the same place of mystery and wonder as Bob Dylan, Joni Mitchell, Bonnie Prince Billy, and Joanna Newsom. His music, which includes settings of numerous poems, has been featured on BBC Radio Oxford Introducing and BBC Radio Cumbria, and he plays regularly in and around Oxford at venues such as The Old Fire Station, The Jericho Tavern, Port Mahon, Oxford Folk Club and Banbury Folk Club, and festivals such as the Banbury Canal Festival and Folk Weekend: Oxford.

about the Oxford University Poetry Society
The Oxford University Poetry Society aims to be the centre of poetic life within the University of Oxford. Established in 1946, OUPS is one of the oldest and most active student poetry societies in the UK, making Oxford a wonderful place for aspiring poets and poetry lovers. In previous years the society has welcomed the likes of Sarah Howe, Don Paterson, Bernard O'Donoghue, Michael Longley, Alice Oswald, Andrew McMillan, Jack Underwood, Kei Miller, Vahni Capildeo, and Mary Jean Chan at events. The society holds workshops, socials, poetry readings, open-mics, competitions, and collaborations with other societies in the works, so come along to as many events as you can. You can read more about OUPS here: https://www.oxforduniversitypoetrysociety.com/

body.content: hello dearoppfriends,

join us in person for our panel entitled ‘intersecting poetry and philosophy’ which will be held onNovember 9th, from 5 to 7 pm, in the Mary Hyde Eccles Room, Pembroke College. invited speakers who work across both genres or integrate cross-disciplinary elements in their work will discuss the resonances of poetic language and philosophy, including subjects such as religious and spiritual philosophical verse traditions, the intersection of literature and philosophy, philosophical poetry, the nature of poetic language, and the links between poetry, philosophy, and other arts.
this event is co-hosted with the Oxford University Poetry Society.
it is part ofopp's fall 2023 event series based on the themes of its fourth journal:1. philosophies of indigenous peoples of northern America2. environment3. sentience

poet Arch Hades, lecturer David Barnes, and philosopher Paul Lodge will discuss aspects of their own research, writing, and creative endeavours engaging in discussion to cultivate an interdisciplinary approach to the subject. the panel will be followed by a musical performance by Paul Lodge and an audience Q&A.
drinks and nibbles will be served starting from 6:20 pm.
we look forward to seeing you there!
hexes,

speaker biosArch Hades is an artist and bestselling poet from London, who became the highest paid living poet in the world in 2021. She is the author of five volumes of poetry – ‘High Tide’ (2018), ‘Fool’s Gold’ (2020), ‘Paper Romance’ (2021), ‘Arcadia’ (2022), ‘21C Human’ (2023) – known best for Romantic lyricism and for exploring poetry-of-philosophy in a traditional, rhyming style.In 2021, ‘Arcadia’ was illustrated and sold as a work of digital fine art at Christie’s New York, where it broke records and became the most expensive poem ever sold at $525,000. In May 2022 the ‘Arcadia’ digital film had its museum premiere at the Palazzo Strozzi in Florence as part of the‘Let’s Get Digital’ exhibition.Arch Hades often writes commentary for national newspapers on cultural discourse, performs poetry readings, and gives talks on philosophy – in 2021 speaking on 21C existentialism at Oxford University; in 2022 exploring the relationship between art and philosophy on the cultural panel at UCL’s annual conference, and discussing the themes of ‘Arcadia’ at the Cambridge Union.

He specialises in Victorian and early twentieth-century writing, with major interests in the city, environmental history, and transatlantic cultural exchange. title: ✩ upcoming events & a new youtube video! ✩
subtitle: a lecture on meditation, a buddhism conference, a seminar on race, and a youtube video on indigenous philosophies!
author: oxfordpublicphilosophy
raw_article:
hello wonderful people,

here are some upcoming, philosophy-related events you might be interested in!

.・。.・゜✭・.・✫・゜・。.

31st october: diri lecture series

a series of three lectures, each considering a distinct problem in the study of early buddhism. The second lecture of their series will take place tomorrow - the theme is spirituality and the topic is meditation.

It will take place at 5pm bst, streamed live on youtube at ‘oxford centre for buddhist studies’

(to attend by zoom, please email steven.egan@ocbs.org)

.・。.・゜✭・.・✫・゜・。.

3rd-5th november: anātman and philosophy conference

this weekend there will be a conference you don’t want to miss! with 23 incredible speakers and 4 faculty supporters, you can attend online or go in person!

this conference seeks to reassess our understanding of buddhist philosophy, and the contemporary discourse it inspires, as well as the views and biases in our historical and present encounters; partaking in this re-examination through a central concept and one of three fundamental marks of phenomenal experience in buddhism: anātman.

read more and register now at anatmanconference.wordpress.com

.・。.・゜✭・.・✫・゜・。.

6th november: the theory of racelessness

an upcoming seminar, offering an exploration of ideas surrounding ‘race’, human migration, and language. this seminar is hosted by the university of maryland, and the speaker is dr.Sheena Mason - who explores this intersection by drawing from research from her upcoming book: the raceless antiracist: why ending race is the future of anti-racism

the seminar will be at 10am-12pm est, and you can join the zoom event by registering here.

you can read more about her work at: theoryofracelessness.org

.・。.・゜✭・.・✫・゜・。.

finally,

if you did not get the opportunity to join our wonderful conversational panel last week: welcoming the philosophies of the indigenous peoples of north america - you can now watch it on youtube!

What?

a conversational panel discussion to provide space for learning more about these indigenous philosophies and the indigenous peoples of northern America.

Why?

As a group doing work in many global philosophies, we recognise that many non-mainstream philosophies are not found within philosophy departments themselves and also that their study involves transdisciplinary conversation which will ultimately transform our understandings of philosophy and the world. We welcome our audience and interdisciplinary panelists to participate in this spirit.

Who?

Judith Jaxootsú Ramos, M.A.T. (Tlingit from Alaska. Spirituality from a Tlingit Worldview.)

Assistant Professor, University of Alaska Southeast co-curator for the Northwest Coast Hall renovation at the American Museum of Natural History in New York Dr

Getty L. Lustila (Choctaw Nation)

Assistant Teaching Professor of Philosophy Susana Kolb Cadwell DPhil student in Anthropology School of Anthropology and Museum Ethnography, University of Oxford

Dr. Daniel Cooper

Geography and Environmental Studies Lecturer California State University San Marcos

Chair: Dr. Shay Welch

Associate Professor of Philosophy 2020-2021 Carnegie Corporation and Rockefeller Foundation Distinguished Research/Creative Scholar Spelman College This conversational panel took place on the 17th of october, hosted by Oxford Public Philosophy and Oxford Philosophy Society.

.・。.・゜✭・.・✫・゜・。.

thank you for your interest in pushing the boundaries of public philosophy!

and have a great week x

love the opp team

title: opp's term card and upcoming events
author: oxfordpublicphilosophy
raw_article:
hello friends,

welcome to everyone who subscribed to our newsletter at Freshers’ Fair! opp is a space for critically questioning what philosophy is and how we are doing it, both in form and content. we welcome work that investigates received assumptions about the nature and aims of philosophical inquiry, including contributions that question structural norms of currently dominant research programmes.

we are pleased to announce that we will be holding an open session and team meeting on October 19th, 5 pm UK time, over Teams (click here to access the meeting). anyone interested in joining opp’s team is welcome to attend the meeting! we are recruiting for co-organisers, turn 5 journal coordinator, content producers and editors for the open forum and online classroom, treasurer/funding team, communications/outreach team, events team, and audio recorders!

we also have three upcoming events! these are part of opp's fall 2023 event series based on the themes of our fourth journal: philosophies of indigenous peoples of northern America, environment, and sentience.

Lustila (Choctaw Nation, Philosophy, Northeastern University), Susana Kolb Cadwell (DPhil Anthropology, University of Oxford), Dr Daniel Cooper (Geography and Environmental Studies, California State University San Marcos), Judith Jaxootsú Ramos (Assistant Professor, University of Alaska Southeast)

title: our call for submissions is closing soon!
author: oxfordpublicphilosophy
raw_article:
hello friends, 

as our call for submissions is closing soon, oxford public philosophy would like to remind you that you can still submit your philosophical compositions iteration for ‘turn 4’ of our annual philosophy journal. we welcome prosaic, poetic, artistic, a meeting thereof, or other-formly pieces focusing on any one or combination of our turn 4 themes:

1. philosophies of the Indigenous peoples of north America
2. environment
3. sentience

we invite contributing participants to submit poetry, argumentative or creative prose, artwork or anything in between to explore one or many of these sub-themes in dialogical acts to expand philosophy’s field of concern, in form and in content, as we encounter it from our various positions in the world.

we invite submissions in three categories: art, poetry, and prose, though contributors should feel free to work over and around these boundaries. multimedia submissions are welcome.

the deadline for submissions is OCTOBER 1ST 2023.

find out more about the submission guidelines here, or, if you're ready, click on our portal below to submit your piece to our team!

keep in touch

opp is always looking for collaborators, contributors, and team members!

you can write something for the opp blog, including reviews (of books, shows, &c), retorts, and anything else that critically reflects on what philosophy is and how we're doing it.

follow us on social media for more regular updates & interactions!

hexes,
the opp team

author: oxfordpublicphilosophy
raw_article:
opp bi-weekly newsletter

hello friends, 

in this newsletter you'll find:

  1. turn 3 launch - friday 27th january 2023

  2. winter 2023 reading groups

  3. what’s opp in philosophy

1. turn 3 launch - friday 27th january 2023

opp (oxfordpublicphilosophy.com)
& oxford philosophy society
invite you to

the launch event for opp’s third annual journal

TURN 3

to learn from East Asian philosophies
& different philosophical mediums

our curations of lines and spaces
to
orient ourselves

join us 
5:30-7:30pm, Friday 27th January 2023
Eccles Room, Pembroke College

for an evening of film, music, poetry, and philosophical discussion
with drinks, refreshments,

& mingling with reasoning magical beings questioning

in form & content…

facebook event

to transgress, question, analyze, uphold, overturn the use and limits of lines as tools or rules

Further details:

Music by: Paul Lodge
Poetry by: Jordan Walters
Dance film by: Christine Foo Mei Juan & Valerie Lim
Design: Zed Nott & Dilara Koz

2. winter 2023 reading groups

week 1 begins sunday 15th January 2023 

Byung-Chul Han Reading Group

Hilary Term 2023.
Time and Location: 7pm Wednesdays, Weeks 1,3,5,7. Theology faculty graduate common room.
Convenors: Aamir Kaderbhai (aamir.kaderbhai@lincoln.ox.ac.uk) and Jack Sagar.


We will be reading a selection of Byung-Chul Han’s works, starting with The Burnout Society (2015).
A Korean-born German Philosopher, Han’s works cover a broad range of themes — from depression, boredom, love, beauty — but orbit around a concern for the challenges faced by the human-subject living in technology-driven and productivity-centred late-capitalist societies. Han is brilliant at writing short, digestible works of philosophy that reect on the complexities of philosophical traditions (both Western and East Asian) whilst oering practical suggestions for modern life. The Burnout Society considers how an excess of ‘positivity’ in society leads to pathologies of ‘auto-exploitation’ that result in epidemics of burnout — surely a subject of personal importance to any Oxford student!

Our sessions will begin with a short summary of the text we will be discussing. So, while we encourage you to read these very concise and fascinating books, do feel free to show up if you didn’t manage to.

No prior knowledge of German or philosophy is required to participate.

Programme:

  • Week 1: The first five chapters of The Burnout Society (25 pages).

    • Email Aamir Kaderbhai for pdf.

  • Week 3: The last three chapters of The Burnout Society (26 pages).

  • Week 5: Reading TBC (provisionally the first half of Agony and Eros).

  • Week 7: Reading TBC.

Philiminality Oxford Reading Group:
The Philosophy of the Xunzi
Hilary Term 2023
Time and Location: TBA


Overview:
In Hilary term 2023, we will be reading and discussing some chapters of the text attributed to the Confucian philosopher Xunzi (3rd century BCE). Even though Xunzi conceived of himself as the orthodox Confucian of his time, his philosophy was rejected by most of the cultural mainstream throughout imperial history, and Xunzi has become most infamous for his opposition to the Mencian doctrine that human nature fundamentally tends towards the good. However, Xunzi’s text has regained widespread attention and recognition in the 20th and 21st century, and today he is again regarded as one of the most important Confucian philosophers. Xunzi’s philosophy is of significant interest not only because he provided a passionate defense of the Confucian Way against countless attacks of rivaling schools, but also because he learned about and incorporated many of the ideas that those rival schools popularised in his time, thus creating an intriguing synthesis of pre-Qin thought. In four sessions throughout this term, we will be following Xunzi’s thought on the metaphysics of Heaven, the importance of ritual and personal cultivation, human nature, and how it is that humans can gain insight in the Way.
 

Frantz Fanon’s The Wretched of the Earth
Hilary Term 2023
time and Location: TBA


In Hilary Term 2023, we will be reading The Wretched of the Earth (Les damnés de la terre, 1961), written by Frantz Fanon (1925-1961). Fanon was born in Martinique, a colony of the French Empire, and trained as a clinical psychotherapist in France. Inspired by his work as a clinician in Algeria and compatriot of the Algerian independence movement, he left behind a trove of psychiatric and political writings that still animate debate to this day. The Wretched of the Earth contains a detailed examination of the colonial context and a diagnosis of its traumatic effects on the colonized subject, as well as a critical evaluation of nationalist liberation movements and the challenges facing formerly colonized nation-states.

The Faculty of Philosophy is seeking to appoint an Associate Professor or Professor in Indian Philosophy, in association with a Tutorial Fellowship at Lady Margaret Hall. The successful candidate will have an area of specialisation in Indian Philosophy, together with a broad range of philosophical interests. This post is available from 1 September 2023, or as soon as possible thereafter. This is a permanent appointment (subject to an initial probation).

The closing date for applications is 12 noon on 16 January 2023.

Interviews are expected to be held in mid to late February 2023.

Byung-Chul Han Reading GroupHilary Term 2023.Time and Location: 7pm Wednesdays, Weeks 1,3,5,7. Theology faculty graduate common room.Convenors: Aamir Kaderbhai (aamir.kaderbhai@lincoln.ox.ac.uk) and Jack Sagar.

We will be reading a selection of Byung-Chul Han’s works, starting withThe Burnout Society (2015).A Korean-born German Philosopher, Han’s works cover a broad range of themes — from depression, boredom, love, beauty — but orbit around a concern for the challenges faced by the human-subject living in technology-driven and productivity-centred late-capitalist societies. Han is brilliant at writing short, digestible works of philosophy that reect on the complexities of philosophical traditions (both Western and East Asian) whilst oering practical suggestions for modern life.The Burnout Societyconsiders how an excess of ‘positivity’ in society leads to pathologies of ‘auto-exploitation’ that result in epidemics of burnout — surely a subject of personal importance to any Oxford student!Our sessions will begin with a short summary of the text we will be discussing. So, while we encourage you to read these very concise and fascinating books, do feel free to show up if you didn’t manage to.No prior knowledge of German or philosophy is required to participate.Programme:
Week 1: The first five chapters ofThe Burnout Society(25 pages).
Email Aamir Kaderbhai for pdf.
Week 3: The last three chapters ofThe Burnout Society(26 pages).
Week 5: Reading TBC (provisionally the first half ofAgony and Eros).
